name: Deadwood Gulch
short_description: BLM public land near Kellogg offering easy access to quartz and chalcedony specimens in a historic mining region. Free rockhounding with 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Deadwood Gulch is a public BLM rockhounding site in the heart of Idaho's historic silver mining district. Located at gentle elevation of 2,382 feet in Shoshone County, this area provides excellent access for both beginner and experienced rockhounds searching for quality quartz and chalcedony specimens. The site sits within the Coeur d'Alene mining region, where intensive mineral activity has exposed numerous collecting opportunities.

The geology here reflects the Mesozoic-era mineralization that created the famous Coeur d'Alene mining district. Hydrothermal fluids circulating through metasedimentary host rock deposited silica-rich veins and created secondary quartz deposits that weather out into gulches and stream beds. Jasper and chalcedony form in these same hydrothermal environments, making Deadwood Gulch a productive location for collectors seeking quality specimens.

The gentle slope and accessible terrain make this an ideal location for families and newer rockhounds. You will find loose material and weathered specimens in the gulch floor and surrounding hillsides. The combination of public access, straightforward terrain, and productive mineralization makes Deadwood Gulch a reliable choice for a day of collecting near Kellogg.

Collectors report finding well-formed quartz crystals, banded chalcedony, and occasional jasper specimens. The site benefits from ongoing natural weathering that continuously exposes fresh material. Plan for a moderate walk from parking to productive collecting areas.
history: Deadwood Gulch lies within the Coeur d'Alene mining district, one of the richest silver-lead-zinc mining regions in North America. The hydrothermal activity that created the valuable ore deposits also formed extensive quartz and chalcedony veins throughout the area. Modern rockhounders benefit from the same geological processes that attracted miners over a century ago.
tips: Search the floor and banks of the gulch wash where water action concentrates heavier specimens and exposes fresh material. Use a small pry bar and chisel to carefully remove specimens from partially weathered outcrops on the hillsides flanking the gulch. Look for translucent quartz with good terminations and banded chalcedony showing white to gray coloring with subtle color banding. Bring a 5-gallon bucket to stay under the 25 lb daily limit while collecting.
terrain_notes: Elevation 2,382 feet with gentle slopes throughout the collecting area. Terrain consists of weathered rocky hillsides surrounding a central gulch with a small wash. Footing is generally stable with scattered loose rock and angular debris. Walking distance from typical parking is less than half mile to productive areas. Light forest vegetation with scattered larger trees. Terrain suitable for most hikers with moderate stability.
best_season: May - September. This mountainous area at moderate elevation experiences winter snow and wet conditions October through April. Summer months provide the best access and most comfortable collecting conditions. Late May through early September offers reliable weather and accessible terrain.
written_directions: From Kellogg, take US Highway 10 west for approximately 3 miles toward Wallace. Watch for Forest Road indicators and turn north on a local access road (coordinates provided: 47.537635, -116.151013 serve as your destination waypoint). Follow the access road to parking pullouts near the gulch entrance. GPS navigation is recommended for precise location.
road_conditions: Access road may be unpaved and rough seasonally. High clearance not required but rough road surface possible. Check conditions after heavy rain or snow.
parking_notes: Small unpaved pullout area near gulch entrance with capacity for 2-3 vehicles. No developed facilities. Parking is informal but established.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or power tools. Hand tools and manual removal only. Specimens must be removed by hand or with non-powered hand tools.
rules:
Personal use only
25 lb maximum per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Remove specimens by hand or non-powered hand tools only
nearest_services: Kellogg (3 miles) offers gas, food, lodging, and supplies. Wallace (6 miles) also provides full services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes throughout accessible to most hikers. Unpaved road to parking area but no special vehicle required. Parking area is informal and near collecting zone. Stable footing on established paths. Suitable for families with school-age children and most mobility levels.

faq.question: What gems can you find rockhounding at Deadwood Gulch near Kellogg Idaho?
faq.answer: Deadwood Gulch is productive for quartz crystals, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. Most collectors find loose specimens in the gulch floor and weathered fragments on surrounding hillsides. The area's hydrothermal history created extensive silica deposits making quartz and chalcedony the most reliable finds.

faq.question: Is Deadwood Gulch free to rockhound and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Deadwood Gulch is completely free to rockhound. No permit is required for recreational collecting on this BLM public land. You only need to follow the 25 lb daily limit and personal use only rules established by the Bureau of Land Management.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Deadwood Gulch?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Deadwood Gulch runs from May through September. This timeframe avoids winter snow and wet conditions while providing comfortable weather and reliable access. Summer months offer the most stable terrain and easiest collecting conditions.

faq.question: Is Deadwood Gulch rockhounding site beginner friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, Deadwood Gulch is ideal for beginners and families. The gentle terrain, easy access from parking, and productive loose material make it perfect for newer rockhounds. No technical climbing or difficult hiking is required to find quality specimens.

faq.question: What tools do you need for rockhounding at Deadwood Gulch Idaho?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar or chisel, gloves, a 5-gallon bucket or backpack for carrying specimens, and sturdy boots. Hand tools are required as motorized equipment is prohibited. A field guide to Idaho minerals and a GPS unit are helpful additions.

faq.question: How far is Deadwood Gulch from Kellogg and what are driving directions?
faq.answer: Deadwood Gulch is approximately 3 miles west of Kellogg via US Highway 10. Take Highway 10 west toward Wallace and watch for local access road signs. The drive takes about 5-10 minutes from downtown Kellogg. GPS coordinates 47.537635, -116.151013 will guide you precisely to the site.

meta_description: Free public BLM rockhounding near Kellogg Idaho. Find quartz, chalcedony and jasper. No permit needed. Easy terrain perfect for families.
